title: Monitoring Comprehension
description: Assessment of the concept that skilled readers notice confusion and apply a specific fix-up strategy.

questions.prompt: What does it mean to monitor comprehension while reading?

choices.text: To notice when meaning breaks down and respond with a strategy
choices.feedback: This is correct because monitoring comprehension means recognizing confusion and taking action to fix it.

choices.text: To read at the same speed no matter how confusing the text becomes
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because monitoring comprehension requires adjustment when understanding weakens.

choices.text: To wait until a quiz to see whether the text made sense
choices.feedback: Monitoring comprehension happens during reading, not only after reading is finished.

choices.text: To highlight every sentence so nothing is missed
choices.feedback: Highlighting everything is not a focused strategy for identifying and repairing confusion.

questions.prompt: A reader reaches the sentence "The policy increased access, but participation still fell" and realizes the two ideas seem to clash. What is the best first response?

choices.text: Skip the sentence and hope the ending explains it
choices.feedback: Skipping confusion does not show monitoring comprehension.

choices.text: Reread the sentence and surrounding lines to identify the source of confusion
choices.feedback: This is correct because an effective first step is to locate the confusion clearly before choosing a fix-up strategy.

choices.text: Replace the sentence with a personal opinion
choices.feedback: Opinion does not help resolve textual confusion.

choices.text: Assume the author made a mistake and stop reading
choices.feedback: A skilled reader should first investigate the confusion using evidence from the text.

questions.prompt: A student does not understand the word scarce in a paragraph about water shortages. Which strategy best shows monitoring comprehension?

choices.text: Ignore the word because one word never matters
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because difficult vocabulary can block understanding and should be addressed.

choices.text: Use nearby clues about limited water supply to infer the word's meaning
choices.feedback: This is correct because the reader notices confusion and applies the vocabulary-in-context strategy to repair meaning.

choices.text: Copy the sentence into notes without thinking about it
choices.feedback: Copying alone does not resolve confusion.

choices.text: Decide the paragraph is unimportant and move on
choices.feedback: Monitoring comprehension requires purposeful action, not avoidance.

questions.prompt: Which response best analyzes a comprehension problem and matches it to an effective fix-up strategy?

choices.text: I do not understand the text, so I will read faster to get it over with.
choices.feedback: Reading faster usually increases confusion instead of repairing it.

choices.text: I lost track of the main point, so I will stop and identify the central idea before continuing.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the reader names the exact problem and selects a strategy that addresses it directly.

choices.text: I saw an unfamiliar example, so I will focus only on punctuation marks.
choices.feedback: Punctuation focus does not match the stated comprehension problem.

choices.text: I feel uncertain, so I will rewrite the article from memory.
choices.feedback: That response does not directly target the source of confusion in the text.

questions.prompt: True or False: Monitoring comprehension means a reader should choose a fix-up strategy that matches the specific confusion in the text.

choices.feedback: This is correct because effective monitoring requires matching the strategy to the actual comprehension problem.
This is incorrect because a fix-up strategy should be chosen based on the kind of confusion the reader notices.
